coredata.dc:title: Rational design of long wavelength solid-state fluorescent carbon dots based on zirconium
coredata.dc:creator: Gong, Xiao
Wu, Yunhui
Li, Jiurong
Zhang, Shiwei
Wu, Min
coredata.dc:description: As a new type of zero dimensional carbon based fluorescent nanomaterial, carbon dots (CDs) have been studied and developed for many years. Various methods have been explored to prepare multicolor CDs to achieve tunable fluorescence of CDs. However, synthesizing fluorescent CDs located in the long wavelength emission region remains a challenge. Here, through a simple one-step solvothermal method using anhydrous citric acid (CA) as the raw material, ZrCl4 as both a metal element dopant and an inorganic salt matrix, and NH3·H2O as nitrogen source, Zr-doped CDs (Zr-CDs) solution with long wavelength fluorescence emission and solid powders with blue fluorescence can be obtained simultaneously. By optimizing the usage of ZrCl4, the fluorescence emission of the prepared CDs red shifted from 596 nm to 650 nm. The introduction of Zr4+ can cause a significant redshift. In addition, based on the sensitivity of CDs to oxygen, they can be used for the detection of trace amounts of H2O2. When using metal-organic framework (MOF) to encapsulate the Zr-CDs which is in the solution, optically stable CD@MOF powders can be obtained, which can be used for the construction of red light-emitting diodes (LEDs), demonstrating its potential application in the field of solid-state lighting. The synthesis technology is expected to provide favorable technical support for the future development of CDs.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the synthesis process of CDs.

(a) The TEM image of p-CDs, and the HRTEM image of the corresponding CDs in the upper right corner, (b) the TEM image of Zr-CDs, and the HRTEM image of the corresponding CDs in the upper right corner, (c) the particle size distribution of p-CDs, (d) the particle size distribution of Zr-CDs.
(a) FTIR spectra of p-CDs and Zr-CDs; (b) FTIR spectra of CD@MOF and B-CDs.

(a–c) The XPS full spectrum, C 1s high-resolution spectrum and N 1s high-resolution spectrum of p-CDs, respectively. (d–f) The XPS full spectrum, C 1s high-resolution spectrum and N 1s high-resolution spectrum of Zr-CDs, respectively.
(a–f) The excitation-emission spectra of p-CDs, Zr-CDs-1, Zr-CDs-2, Zr-CDs-3, Zr-CDs-4, and Zr-CDs-5, respectively.

(a) CIE coordinates and optical spectrum of red LEDs; Emission spectra of (b) red LEDs under 20 mA, 40 mA, 60 mA, 80 mA and 100 mA. Fingerprint identification: The collection of fingerprints using Zr-CDs (c1) and CD@MOF (c2).
(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of different volumes of H2O2 in Zr-CDs (excited at 365 nm); (b) Fitting diagram of peak fluorescence intensity and the amount of H2O2.

head.ce:title: Rational design of long wavelength solid-state fluorescent carbon dots based on zirconium

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As a new type of zero dimensional carbon based fluorescent nanomaterial, carbon dots (CDs) have been studied and developed for many years. Various methods have been explored to prepare multicolor CDs to achieve tunable fluorescence of CDs. However, synthesizing fluorescent CDs located in the long wavelength emission region remains a challenge. Here, through a simple one-step solvothermal method using anhydrous citric acid (CA) as the raw material, ZrCl4 as both a metal element dopant and an inorganic salt matrix, and NH3·H2O as nitrogen source, Zr-doped CDs (Zr-CDs) solution with long wavelength fluorescence emission and solid powders with blue fluorescence can be obtained simultaneously. By optimizing the usage of ZrCl4, the fluorescence emission of the prepared CDs red shifted from 596 nm to 650 nm. The introduction of Zr4+ can cause a significant redshift. In addition, based on the sensitivity of CDs to oxygen, they can be used for the detection of trace amounts of H2O2. When using metal-organic framework (MOF) to encapsulate the Zr-CDs which is in the solution, optically stable CD@MOF powders can be obtained, which can be used for the construction of red light-emitting diodes (LEDs), demonstrating its potential application in the field of solid-state lighting. The synthesis technology is expected to provide favorable technical support for the future development of CDs.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), as a highly anticipated new star in the carbon family, are mainly composed of sp2/sp3 carbon skeletons and abundant functional groups/polymer chains [1–5]. The discovery of CDs has aroused great interest among researchers since it was unexpectedly discovered in 2004 due to it inherits the environmentally friendly, low/non-toxic characteristics of carbon materials [6]. Compared with traditional fluorescent materials, such as organic fluorescent dyes, metal nanoparticles/clusters, semiconductor quantum dots, etc., CDs possess unique optical properties and other advantages (such as the tunability of photoluminescence (PL), the adjustable of photoluminescence quantum yields (PLQYs), the wide range of raw materials, and the ease of preparation), so it is considered as potential substitutes for multicolor luminescent materials [7–16]. The controllable preparation of CDs is a prerequisite for scientific research and practical application, and this can be achieved by selecting different synthesis methods [17–19]. Up to now, there are two main methods for preparing CDs, namely top-down and bottom-up [20,21]. Although the top-down method was discovered early, the operation is generally cumbersome and complex, and the quantum yield of CDs produced and the efficiency are low, which is not suitable for large-scale preparation. The bottom-up method requires simple equipment which can generate high fluorescence quantum yield and realize large-scale production [22–25]. Although the bottom-up method is simple and efficient, the emission of most of the CDs synthesized by this method is mainly blue or green fluorescence located in the short wavelength region [26–29]. In order to obtain long-wavelength luminescent CDs, researchers have put in unremitting efforts on the way forward.

At present, most reports on the use of dopants to promote CDs redshift use non-metallic elements as dopants, and there are few reports on the use of metallic elements as dopants for emission in the long wavelength region. Compared with most non-metallic atoms, most metal ions (especially transition metal ions) have more electrons and unoccupied orbits, and their atomic radius is also larger [30]. After the CDs are doped with metal ions, their electron density distribution and energy gap change dramatically, which can adjust the physical and chemical properties of the CDs [31]. And also, the metal ions introduced by doping metal ions may chelate with the electrophilic groups of CDs, thereby changing the electronic environment inside the CDs and regulating their optical properties. For example, He et al. synthesized Zn-CQDs with high photoluminescence properties via the introduction of zinc citrate chelate as precursors, and the fluorescence intensity of Zn-CQDs was eight times higher than that of undoped with zinc ions CQDs [32]. Incredibly, many people believe that red CDs usually contain a large conjugated carbon nucleus, so most reports on red CDs use aromatic molecules as precursors in their synthesis processes. Contrarily, there are few reports on the synthesis of red CDs using non-aromatic compounds as precursors. Citric acid (CA), as a non-aromatic precursor, has abundant functional groups (-COOH and –OH) and reaction sites, and carbon nanostructures can be obtained through crosslinking and carbonization of CA [33,34]. To date, most of the reported fluorescence emission from CDs is limited to solution luminescence, while CDs will suffer from aggregation-induced fluorescence weakening or even fluorescence quenching dilemmas when it is in the solid state. The quenching phenomenon caused by aggregation is usually described as the decrease of loss the fluorescence intensity or quenching of fluorescence due to their interactions that occur in high-concentration solutions or solids. In order to effectively avoid the fluorescence problems suffered by CDs in the solid state and to realize its wider versatility, researchers have adopted a series of approaches to achieve solid-state luminescence of CDs. Specifically, some studies have demonstrated that inorganic salt matrices and metal oxides can be applied as solid matrices to realize solid-state luminescence of CDs, the use of these matrices can not only maintain the optical properties of CDs but also enhance its luminescent properties [35].

In this paper, we synthesized red Zr-doped CDs (Zr-CDs) with fluorescence emission wavelength exceeding 650 nm through a one-step solvothermal method employing CA as the carbon source, ammonia water (NH3·H2O) as the nitrogen source, N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF) as the reaction solvent, and ZrCl4 as an auxiliary inorganic salt and dopant. The results showed that adding ZrCl4 could produce a significant redshift in the fluorescence emission wavelength of Zr-CDs from the original 575 nm–650 nm compared to the CDs without adding ZrCl4. The addition of ZrCl4 could improve the degree of crosslinking and carbonization of CDs, thereby affecting the particle size of CDs and the content of graphite nitrogen, and ultimately obtaining CDs solution with long wavelength emission. The introduction of ZrCl4 not only caused a redshift in the emission peak of the CDs solution, but also generated solid powder accompanied by blue fluorescence, achieving the one-step synthesis of solid CDs. The combination of Zr-CDs with metal-organic framework (MOF) also enabled the red solid-state fluorescence emission of Zr-CDs@MOF composites. In addition, due to the special properties of Zr-CDs, it could be used to detect trace amounts of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2). Also, by mixing the Zr-CDs@MOF with AB adhesive, combined with a 365 nm chip, red light-emitting diodes (LEDs) could be fabricated. This work is expected to provide favorable technical support for the future synthesis of solid-state luminescence of CDs and effective composite with MOF, paving the way for the development of CDs.

Zirconium Chloride (ZrCl4), Zinc Acetate ((CH3CO2)2Zn), Citric Acid (CA), 1,2-Dimethylimidazole, and Poly(vinyl alcohol) (PVA) were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd., China. Citrate Ammonia solution (NH3·H2O), N,N-Dimethylformamide (DMF), Hydrogen Peroxide (H2O2) and Ethanol (EtOH) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemistry Reagent Co., Ltd., China.

Preparation of the pristine CDs (p-CDs): 0.96 g anhydrous CA was weighed and mixed evenly in 10 mL DMF, and then 1 mL NH3·H2O was added. After ultrasonic treatment for 10 min, the fully mixed solution was transferred to a reaction kettle with a volume of 25 mL and reacted at 180 °C for 4 h. Then, 0.22 μm filtration membrane was used to filter and purify the reaction products. Subsequently, the reaction products were further purified using dialysis bags with 1000 Da interception molecular weight, and the dialysis time was 24 h.

Preparation of Zr-CDs: The preparation method was basically the same as that of p-CDs, except that 0.6 g ZrCl4 was added as a dopant and inorganic salt substrate in the preparation process. After the reaction, the solution was purified by 0.22 μm filtration membrane to obtain Zr-CDs. The remaining solid part was placed in the oven at 50 °C for drying with 48 h, and the black solid powder with blue light was obtained, which is denoted as B-CDs. Subsequently, the doses of different ZrCl4 were varied (0, 0.2, 0.4, 0.6, 0.8, and 1.0 g) and other experimental conditions remained unchanged, and an experimental control group was set up.

Preparation of CD@MOF: 3 g of Zn(COOH)2 was added to 100 mL of deionized water to configure the A solution, and then 8 g of 1,2-dimethylimidazole was added to 100 mL of deionized water to configure the B solution. Respectively, 5 mL of solution A and solution B was taken, respectively, which was mixed to form a clear suspension (such suspension were taken as 15 aliquots each), and immediately 0.05 mL, 0.10 mL, 0.15 mL, 0.20 mL, 0.25 mL, 0.30 mL, 0.35 mL, 0.40 mL, 0.45 mL, 0.50 mL, 0.55 mL, 0.60 mL, 0.65 mL, 0.70 mL, 0.75 mL of Zr-CDs suspension was added, respectively. After the mixture was evenly mixed and placed for 12 h under magnetic stirring. Finally, the mixture was centrifuged (6000 rpm, 5 min) and washed three times with anhydrous ethanol and deionized water respectively. The obtained solid powder was dried at 40 °C with 48 h. The obtained solid powders were labeled as from CD@MOF-1 to CD@MOF-15, respectively.

CD@MOF-11 powder was encapsulated using epoxy resin (with a volume ratio of 10:3 between adhesive A and adhesive B), mixed in a 1:1 powder to adhesive ratio, and a 365 nm (1 W) chip was selected for simple encapsulation to construct red LEDs.

120 KV transmission electron microscopy (TEM model: JEM-1400Plus/JEM-1400Plus) was used for morphology and structure characterization. X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS model: ESCALAB 250Xi/ESCALAB 250Xi) was utilized for elemental and compositional analysis. X-ray diffractometer (XRD) was applied for lattice structure analysis. Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR model: Nicolet6700/Nicolet6700) and Raman spectra (LABHRev-UV) were utilized for composition and structure analysis. UV–visible near infrared spectrophotometer (Lambda 750 S/Lambda 750 S) was used to characterize the optical properties. The PL spectra of the LEDs were characterized by a Spectrascan Model PR-650 spectrophotometer with an integral sphere.

By adopting a one-step solvothermal method, anhydrous CA as the carbon source, NH3·H2O as the nitrogen source, ZrCl4 as the dopant, and DMF as the solvent, red-emitting Zr-CDs solution and blue-emitting B-CDs solid powders could be obtained simultaneously after reaction in 180 °C for 4 h.

The synthesis process is shown in Fig. 1 . ZrCl4 not only promoted the redshift of the CDs fluorescence emission throughout the entire reaction process but also served as an inorganic salt matrix to realize solid-state luminescence. To verify this phenomenon, a control experiment without ZrCl4 as a dopant was conducted (referred to as p-CDs) and the remaining experimental conditions of undoped and doped CDs were identical. Zr-CDs exhibited the strongest fluorescence emission at 650 nm, and black solid powders exhibited blue fluorescence emission at 450 nm. However, p-CDs only exhibited the strongest emission peak at around 575 nm, and there was no solid formation. Obviously, Zr-CDs not only exhibited a redshift of 75 nm relative to the strongest emission peak of p-CDs but also obtained solid CDs powders with blue fluorescence emission that could be directly obtained through centrifugal drying. This indicates that ZrCl4 plays an essential role in the synthesis of red emission CDs solution and its solid-state emission powders. The corresponding controlled experiments at different ZrCl4 doses are shown in Fig. S1. There was no solid state production of CDs in the absence of ZrCl4. The solution fluorescence of CDs was gradually red-shifted with increasing ZrCl4, reaching an optimum at a dose of 0.6 g ZrCl4. Subsequently, the fluorescence of CDs gradually diminished with continued increase in the dose of ZrCl4. The corresponding yield of solid-state CDs was increasing with the increase of ZrCl. Based on the control experiments at different ZrCl4 doses, it was found that the yield of CDs at 0.6 g ZrCl4 was 24.35 %.

The structures of p-CDs and Zr-CDs were tested using TEM. As shown in Fig. 2 a and b, the TEM images showed that both p-CDs and Zr-CDs exhibited good dispersion and their sizes were uniform. A random selection of 120 locations was made for statistical analysis of the particle size distribution of CDs. The particle size distribution diagram is shown in Fig. 2c and d. The particle size distribution of p-CDs ranged from 2.2 to 6.5 nm, with an average size of 4.14 nm. The particle size distribution of Zr-CDs ranged from 2.8 to 10.8 nm, with an average size of 5.22 nm. It can be clearly seen from the figures that the particle size of Zr-CDs was significantly larger than that of p-CDs. The quantum size effect has been proven to affect the fluorescence emission of CDs [36]. As the particle size increases, the fluorescence emission peak positions of CDs undergo a redshift [36]. In addition, it can also be seen from HRTEM that Zr-CDs had clearer lattice stripes, indicating that Zr-CDs might have a higher degree of carbonization. Therefore, it can be inferred that the addition of ZrCl4 will affect the intermolecular amination, deacidification, intermolecular dehydration, and further carbonization processes of CA and NH3·H2O. Furthermore, the particle size of CDs may affect the expansion of the conjugated domain of carbon nuclei, thereby affecting the fluorescence emission of CDs. That is, the emission peak red shifts with the size increase of CDs. Through HRTEM images, it can see that p-CDs and Zr-CDs had similar lattice stripes. Measurement of their lattice spacing reveals that they were both 0.21 nm, which is in good agreement with the graphite carbon (100) crystal plane [37–41]. The results indicate that the core structures of p-CDs and Zr-CDs were similar to those of graphite. To further analyze the structure of CDs, the XRD tests on p-CDs and Zr-CDs were conducted. According to Fig. S2, both CDs exhibited clear and wide peaks at approximately 25°, corresponding to the (002) crystal plane of graphite carbon [42]. This indicates that the interlayer stacking within the CDs is very similar to that of graphite, and the wide peak at around 42° corresponds to the (100) crystal plane of graphite carbon, which can well correspond to their HRTEM images [43–45]. These peaks can well indicate that the prepared CDs had graphite properties [46]. At the same time, the XRD spectra of CD@MOF was performed, as shown in Fig. S2c. It can be found that there were peaks of MOF in Zr-CDs, which proves the existence of MOF in Zr-CDs, that is, Zr-CDs were encapsulated in MOF.

In order to verify the structure and configuration composition of these CDs, FTIR was applied to characterize their chemical structures. FTIR spectroscopy can be used to detect the types of functional groups present in substances.

As shown in Fig. 3 , it can be seen that the absorption peaks observed in both CDs solutions and CDs powders in the range of 3000–3500 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibrations of - OH and N–H, while the peak between 2700 and 3000 cm−1 is attributed to the C–H stretching vibration. As shown in Fig. 3a, the absorption peaks of Zr-CDs and p-CDs near 1620 cm−1 might be caused by the stretching vibration of CO, and the peaks around 1403 cm−1 might be attributed to the stretching vibration of C–N. The absorption peak of around 1230 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of C–O [33]. By comparing the intensity of the peaks in the FTIR spectra of these two CDs solutions, it can be found that with the addition of ZrCl4, CO increased, and an increase in the content of carbonyl carbon happened. The increase in CO may lead to a redshift in the fluorescence emission of CDs. The FTIR spectra of solid CDs powders B-CDs and CD@MOF are shown in Fig. 3b. Apart from having some functional groups similar to those in CDs solution, it can be seen that the peak near 1470 cm−1 can be attributed to CC. It can be observed that its strength increased, which might indicate that the degree of carbonization of CDs encapsulated with ZrCl4 inorganic salt matrix was promoted. By further analysis of Raman spectra of p-CDs and Zr-CDs (Fig. S3), it is evident that they had two characteristic peaks, namely the D-band and G-band, located near 1350 cm−1 and 1580 cm−1, respectively [47,48]. The D band may be caused by defects, while the G band is generated during the stretching motion of sp2 atomic pairs, representing the sp2 hybrid motion of CDs, i.e., graphitization structure [49]. The intensity ratios of G and D bands were calculated to be IG/ID, with IG/ID of 1.01 and 1.05 for p-CDs and Zr-CDs, respectively. The results showed that after the addition of ZrCl4, the IG/ID value of the CDs solution increased, indicating that the degree of graphitization of CDs increased and surface defects decreased.

XPS can further analyze the internal structure and possible surface groups of CDs. Firstly, by analysis of the CDs solution, it can be seen that both CDs solutions contained C, N, and O elements, with characteristic peaks located at 284.8, 400.2, and 531.2 eV (Fig. 4 a and d).

Zr elements were also present in Zr-CDs, with characteristic peaks located at 182.8 eV (about 1.21 % loading of Zr). After analyzing the fine spectra of these two CDs solutions, it was found that the C 1s fine spectra can be separated into three peaks, located at 284.8, 285.8, and 288.8 eV, respectively, corresponding to CC/C–C, C–O/C–N, and CO [50]. From Fig. 4b and e, it can be seen that the CC/C–C strength in Zr-CDs was also slightly higher than that in p-CDs, which may be due to an increase in sp2 carbon, i.e., the degree of graphitization in Zr-CDs was slightly higher than that in p-CDs. The C–O/C–N intensity in Zr-CDs was significantly lower than that in p-CDs, indicating a decrease in sp3 carbon. The significant increase in CO strength indicates an increase in carbonyl carbon content, which corresponds well with the results shown in the FTIR spectrum. Based on the analysis of XPS and FTIR, it can be concluded that the addition of ZrCl4 may lead to a deeper degree of graphitization of CDs and an increase in the number of CO groups. The N 1s spectra of p-CDs and Zr-CDs could be fitted to three characteristic peaks, located at 399.6, 400.8, and 401.5 eV, respectively, corresponding to pyridine nitrogen, pyrrole nitrogen, and graphite nitrogen [51–53]. From Fig. 4c and f, it is evident that the pyridine nitrogen peak intensity of Zr-CDs was significantly decreased compared to p-CDs, and pyridine nitrogen was related to the emission of short wavelength regions. In addition, the relative content of Zr-CDs graphite nitrogen peak was also increased compared to p-CDs, which may be due to the addition of ZrCl4 promoting the generation of graphite nitrogen. Subsequently, peak fitting was performed on the O 1s fine spectra of the two CDs, and the results showed that two characteristic peaks could be distinguished, one attributed to OC and the other to O–C. These two groups were located at 531.8 and 533.3 eV, respectively [54,55]. From Figs. S4a and S4b, it is evident that the O–C intensity in Zr-CDs was significantly lower than that in p-CDs, and the relative content of OC increased, indicating an increase in the degree of oxidation of CDs after the addition of ZrCl4. Finally, Fig. S4c show that Zr 3d can be divided into two characteristic peaks: 182.9 eV and 185.3 eV, which correspond to Zr 3d5/2 (Zr O) and Zr 3d3/2 (Zr O), respectively. Based on the analysis of FTIR, Raman, and XPS spectra of Zr-CDs and p-CDs, it is speculated that the addition of ZrCl4 could promote the increase of carbon particle size and graphite nitrogen content, and decrease the pyridine nitrogen content during the reaction process. Meanwhile, the surface functional groups also changed, that is, the content of CO and CC increased. This indicates that the synergistic effect between the conjugation effect and surface state can promote the redshift of CDs fluorescence emission together.

Further XPS analysis was conducted on the blue CDs powder directly prepared and the Zr-CDs coated with MOF. From Figs. S5a and S5e, it can be seen that the two types of CDs powders, like the solution of Zr-CDs, both contained four elements: C, N, O, and Zr. Their characteristic peak positions were around 284.8, 398.6, 531.6 and 189.6 eV. The C 1s fine spectrum could be fitted to three peaks, located at 284.8, 285.8, and 288.8 eV, corresponding to CC/C–C, C–O/C–N, and CO (Figs. S5b and S5f). The N 1s spectrum could also be fitted to three peaks, located at 399.6 eV, 400.8 eV, and 401.5 eV, corresponding to pyridine nitrogen, pyrrole nitrogen, and graphite nitrogen (Figs. S5c and S5g). The O 1s fine spectrum could be fitted to two characteristic peaks, belonging to OC and O–C, respectively. These two functional groups were located at 531.8 eV and 533.3 eV, respectively (Figs. S5d and S5h). The XPS of B-CDs and R-CDs shows that Zr 3d could be divided into two characteristic peaks: 182.9 eV and 185.3 eV, which correspond to Zr 3d5/2 (Zr–O) and Zr 3d3/2 (Zr–O), respectively (Figs. S6a and S6b) [56]. In addition, there was also a metal element Zn in CD@MOF, with a characteristic peak located at 1021.6 eV, which could be divided into three peaks: 1021.6, 1022.7, and 1045.5 eV, corresponding to Zn–O, Zn–C, and Zn 2p1/2 (Fig. S6c) [57]. The occurrence of Zn is due to the use of Zn(COOH)2 as the raw material for preparing MOF [58,59]. Comparing the XPS spectra of CD@MOF and Zr-CDs, it was found that the content of each component in the same CDs yielded a slight change, which may be due to the influence of MOF raw materials on them, proving the existence of MOF [60].

To investigate the effect of ZrCl4 on the optical properties of CDs, CDs samples with different ZrCl4 usage levels of 0, 0.2, 0.4, 0.6, 0.8, and 1.0 g were prepared. The corresponding CDs samples were named p-CDs, Zr-CDs-1, Zr-CDs-2, Zr-CDs-3, Zr-CDs-4, and Zr-CDs-5, respectively. By observing their absorption spectra, it can be observed that both p-CDs and Zr-CDs exhibited similar absorption peaks, with a strong absorption peak between 250 and 300 nm. This absorption peak is attributed to the π-π* transition of CC in the sp2 hybrid carbon nucleus and generally does not produce fluorescence [61,62]. In addition, they all exhibited a broad sub-strong absorption peak around 350 nm, which may be attributed to the n-π* transition of CO [63,64]. At the same time, they also showed a clear absorption peak near 525 nm and 570 nm, which may be attributed to transitions related to surface states (Fig. S7) [65,66]. Meanwhile, the optimal excitation and emission wavelengths of the original sample p-CDs and the optimal sample Zr-CDs were analyzed. It can be found that their optimal excitation and emission wavelengths were 580/585 nm and 578/638 nm, respectively. Obviously, the optimal fluorescence emission of Zr-CDs had a significant redshift compared to p-CDs and exceeded 50 nm. In order to better study the luminescence characteristics of CDs, the relationship between the excitation and emission of CDs was systematically studied. As shown in the figure, both p-CDs and Zr-CDs exhibited similar excitation-independent PL emission, meaning that their PL emission peaks did not change with the change of excitation wavelength. However, it can also be found that although Zr-CDs showed a nearly independent excitation emission relationship, there was a slight shift in the high-energy excited state, that is, under the high excitation of 608 nm, it showed an emission of about 650 nm, which may be related to the surface state.

In addition, by analyzing and comparing the fluorescence emission peaks of five samples, Zr-CDs-1, Zr-CDs-2, Zr-CDs-3, Zr-CDs-4, and Zr-CDs-5, it was found that under the same reaction time and temperature, as the amount of ZrCl4 increased, their fluorescence emission peaks gradually red shifted. When the amount of ZrCl4 used was 0.2 g, it was found that the fluorescence emission peaks of CDs had two peaks, located at 596 nm and 626 nm, respectively.

As the excitation wavelength increased, the emission peak at 596 nm initially decreased, while the emission peak at 626 nm increased. However, when the emission peak position increased to 508 nm, the intensity of the 596 nm peak position began to increase, forming the strongest emission peak position. When the usage of ZrCl4 continued to increase, the peak position at 596 nm disappeared and the peak at 626 nm shifted to 634 nm when the usage was 0.4 g. The emission peak also exhibited excitation-independent properties. When the amount of ZrCl4 used was 0.6 g, it can be found that the fluorescence emission of the CDs had a further redshift and showed a 650 nm long wavelength emission under the 608 nm high energy excited state. Of course, it can also be clearly observed that when the amount of ZrCl4 increased to around 0.6 g, further increasing the amount of ZrCl4 resulted in little change in the fluorescence emission peak of the prepared CDs (Fig. 5 ).

Therefore, it is believed that the CDs prepared using 0.6 g ZrCl4 are the best sample. This sample was encapsulated with MOF material and its optical properties were investigated. As shown in Fig. S8, the optical images of CD@MOF-7 to CD@MOF-14 under sunlight and 365 nm UV lamp under different CDs amounts are presented. As shown in Fig. S9e, it can be well seen that under a unified 365 nm excitation, the peak at 625 nm of CD@MOF-11 was the highest and the peak at 436 nm was the lowest. Therefore, it is judged that CD@MOF-11 was the optimal sample. And also, it can be seen that the amount of CDs coated in MOF affected the intensity of the blue emission at around 436 nm and red emission at around 625 nm. But they all showed similar fluorescence emission spectra, with fluorescent photos of Zr-CDs encapsulated with MOFs of different Zr-CDs and images under 365 nm UV lamp as shown in Fig. S8. In addition, it was also found that the solid left over from the preparation of Zr-CDs was dried, and the resulting solid powder showed black under sunlight and dark blue under 365 nm UV lamp irradiation. The test results of its fluorescence properties revealed that its optimal excitation and emission wavelengths were 370/438 nm, and its fluorescence spectrum showed that the directly obtained solid powder had excitation independence (Fig. S10a). In contrast, the introduction of MOF generated a new emission peak near 625 nm (Fig. S10b), which may originate from the excellent fixation of the emission of Zr-CDs by the framework structure of MOF. At the same time, the fluorescence lifetimes of p-CDs and Zr-CDs were measured. Their time-resolved PL decay curves are shown in Fig. S11. A double exponential function was used to fit them, indicating that there are multiple attenuation channels. The fluorescence lifetime of p-CDs was 2.39 ns, Zr-CDs was 2.01 ns, B-CDs was 4.44 ns, and CD@MOF was 2.96 ns. Obviously, the fluorescence lifetime of Zr-CDs after MOF was encapsulated was higher than that of the initial Zr-CDs, which indicated that MOF had a good fluorescence fixation effect. To measure the value of CD@MOF in practical applications, the stability of CD@MOF powder was tested. As shown in Fig. S12, the prepared CD@MOF were placed in a natural environment for 6 months, and their fluorescence performance was tested. Compared with the fluorescence spectra of the newly prepared CD@MOF, it was found that the fluorescence emission peak positions remained almost unchanged, which proves that the Zr-CDs composite material has excellent optical stability. At the same time, SEM tests have been conducted on CD@MOF. As shown in Fig. S13, CD@MOF shows the morphology of nanoflowers, which may be caused by the prepared type of MOFs being MOF–ON–MOF [67,68].

Due to the advantages of good stability, simple preparation method, easy processing, low cost, and low toxicity of Zr-CDs encapsulated with MOF, CD@MOF-11 powder with good PL performance was selected to construct monochromatic LEDs to evaluate their practical applications. The prepared CD@MOF-11 powders was mixed with AB adhesive and encapsulated on an InGaN chip emitting 365 nm to prepare red LEDs devices.

As shown in Fig. 6 , by observing the optical images of red LEDs prepared under a working current of 20 mA and corresponding Commission Internationale d'Eclairage (CIE) spectra, it can be well observed that the prepared red LEDs had similar fluorescence emission peaks to the Zr-CDs solution, and their CIE coordinates were located at (0.42, 0.26). In addition, its fluorescence emissions were tested at different currents. When the current was 20 mA, 40 mA, 60 mA, 80 mA, 100 mA, and the voltage was 3 V, the fluorescence emission peak position hardly changed with the change of current, and the intensity only slightly changed. This indicates that the prepared red LEDs have good stability and great potential in LED applications. Additionally, since CDs themselves are abundant in surface functional groups, they can interact with oils or proteins, and potential fingerprinting can be accomplished easily and quickly. Fig. 6c demonstrates the utilization of Zr-CDs and CD@MOF powders to achieve the contribution to fingerprint identification. The patterns between the fingerprints, including wrinkles, crosses, rings and grooves, can be clearly observed in Fig. 6c. This indicates that solid powders of CDs have potential applications in fingerprint identification.

H2O2 has attracted much attention in food science, environmental protection, chemistry, mining, pharmaceutical and other fields, and it has also been used as a disinfectant for pools, and food and beverage packaging. In addition, H2O2 acts as a strong oxidant during the formation of sulfuric acid from sulfur dioxide, leading to an increase in the acidity of rainwater. Excess H2O2 content in organisms may trigger several signal proteins that are necessary to affect cell proliferation, thus leading to various diseases, such as cancer, diabetes, and cardiovascular and neurodegenerative diseases. Usually, H2O2, as a mild substance, can produce bubbles in the stomach when swallowed excessively, causing significant irritation to the stomach and even burning tissue. The excessive use of H2O2 in industry and nuclear power plants has greatly affected the environment. Considering the harmfulness of H2O2 and biological damage such as ageing, neurodegeneration, cancer, as well as its irritating effects on organs such as the eyes, from biological analysis to pharmaceuticals and environmental protection, the detection of H2O2 is crucial [69]. Several methods for detecting H2O2 have been reported, including spectroscopic, electrochemical, fluorescence, and colorimetric methods. However, nanoparticle fluorescent probes have better spatial resolution than electrochemical methods.

CDs are considered as a suitable material for fluorescent probes due to their low toxicity, good biocompatibility, and solubility. Compared with traditional detection methods, fluorescence analysis has the advantages of high sensitivity, fast response speed, simple operation, and easy portability. Many articles have been reported on the application of CDs in the field of fluorescence probes, for example, CDs can be used to detect Ca2+, Cr3+, tetracycline, flaxanthine and so on [70–73]. Of course, there have been articles reported about detecting H2O2. Although fluorescent materials have been widely used for the detection of H2O2, there are still some shortcomings that need to be optimized. Specifically, many fluorescent probes lack good biocompatibility.

Considering that the fluorescence of Zr-CDs may change after prolonged exposure to the environment and that it may be due to oxygen sensitivity and oxidation in the environment, different amounts of H2O2 diluents (diluted 10 times with 30 % H2O2 solution) were added to the same volume of CDs solution (2 mL) for fluorescence testing.

As shown in Fig. 7 , under the same excitation wavelength, the fluorescence emission peak position remained almost unchanged, but the intensity changed significantly, this indicates that the Zr-CDs have the ability to detect H2O2. Its fluorescence intensity with the amount of H2O2 used was fitted, and the fitting curve was: y = 0.45854*exp(-x/8.18688) + 0.46289*exp(-x/8.18697) +0.0846, R2 = 0.99513. Furthermore, based on the average value of 10 blank samples plus three times the average standard deviation, the limit of detection (LOD) was calculated [74]. Compared with previous reports of CDs-based applications for H2O2 (Table S1), the as-synthesized Zr-CDs are more potentially advantageous for H2O2 detection. This indicates that it has good application prospects in detecting H2O2.

In summary, a simple and effective method has been developed to synthesize red fluorescence emitting Zr-CDs by using anhydrous CA as the precursor, ZrCl4 as both a metal ion dopant and an inorganic salt matrix. After adding ZrCl4, it was found that the size of CDs significantly increased, the degree of graphitization deepened, and the fluorescence emission of the prepared CDs changed from orange to red. It can be found that the introduction of Zr4+ could cause a significant redshift in the fluorescence emission peak position of CDs, and could also produce specific CD powders with blue emission. In addition, due to the sensitivity of the prepared CDs to oxygen, they could be applied to the detection of H2O2. When MOF was used to encapsulate with Zr-CDs, the stability of CD@MOF was significantly improved which could be applied to fabricate monochromatic LEDs. This synthesis technique is expected to provide favorable technical support for the development of CDs in the future.
